sections.heading: The English alphabet
sections.body: English uses the Latin alphabet with 26 letters. Vowels (a, e, i, o, u) often change sound in unstressed syllables. Consonant clusters like 'th', 'sh', and 'ch' are common.
sections.learnerTip: Read aloud daily — spelling and sound do not always match in English.

sections.heading: Stress and rhythm
sections.body: English is stress-timed: content words (nouns, verbs) are louder and longer. Function words (the, of, to) are reduced.
examples: I WANT to GO to SCHOOL.

chapters.title: Nouns & articles
chapters.summary: Count vs mass nouns and the articles a, an, the.

sections.heading: Articles
sections.body: Use 'a/an' for one non-specific thing; use 'the' when both speaker and listener know which thing is meant.
sections.learnerTip: If you can point to it, 'the' is often correct.

sections.heading: Simple present
sections.body: Add -s/-es for he/she/it: I work, she works. Use for habits and facts.

sections.heading: Present continuous
sections.body: am/is/are + -ing for actions happening now or temporary situations.

sections.heading: Questions
sections.body: Invert subject and auxiliary: Do you…? Is she…? Wh-words go first: What do you want?

sections.heading: Negation
sections.body: Use do not / does not + base verb, or not after be/have auxiliaries.

sections.heading: Position
sections.body: Adjectives usually come before nouns: a red car. After linking verbs: The car is red.

sections.heading: Comparatives
sections.body: Short adjectives: -er (taller) or more + adj (more beautiful). Superlative: the tallest / the most beautiful.

sections.heading: Subject vs object
sections.body: I/we/you/they/he/she/it as subjects; me/us/you/them/him/her/it as objects.

chapters.title: Past & future
chapters.summary: Simple past and common future forms.

sections.heading: Simple past
sections.body: Regular verbs: -ed. Irregular verbs have unique forms (go → went). Time words: yesterday, last week.

sections.heading: Future
sections.body: will + verb for decisions/predictions; going to + verb for plans.
examples: We are going to travel tomorrow.

sections.heading: Politeness
sections.body: Please, thank you, excuse me, and could you… soften requests.
sections.learnerTip: Please and thank you are expected in service situations.

sections.heading: Prepositions of place & time
sections.body: in/on/at for time and place: in 2026, on Monday, at 5 pm; in the room, on the table, at home.
